summary: German composer (1813–1883), known primarily for his operatic works and the concept of the Gesamtkunstwerk (total work of art). Major works include Tristan und Isolde, the Ring Cycle, Die Meistersinger von Nürnberg, Tannhäuser, Lohengrin, and Parsifal. Founded the Bayreuth Festival. Wrote the antisemitic essay 'Jewishness in Music.' Had a famous falling out with Friedrich Nietzsche. Influenced Bruckner and was admired by Hitler.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: German composer known for his concept of the Gesamtkunstwerk (total work of art), leitmotifs, and music dramas. Father-in-law of Franz Liszt (Cosima was Liszt's daughter and Wagner's wife). His son-in-law Houston Stewart Chamberlain wrote The Foundations of the Nineteenth Century. His essays include 'The Artwork of the Future,' 'Art and Revolution,' 'Opera and Drama,' and the antisemitic 'Jewishness in Music.' Wikipedia

examples: This philosopher outlined his concept of the 'total work of art' in that same work.
This thinker's Opera and Drama elaborated the ideas of gesamtkunstwerk and leitmotiv that were later used to write his Ring Cycle.

clues.clue: Attacked Giacomo Meyerbeer, Felix Mendelssohn, and Jewish people in the essay 'Jewishness in Music'
examples: This composer of an opera based on an epic by Wolfram von Eschenbach also wrote the antisemitic essay 'Jewishness in Music.'
This philosopher attacked Giacomo Meyerbeer, Felix Mendelssohn, and Jewish people in general in his essay 'Jewishness in Music.'

clues.clue: Relationship with Friedrich Nietzsche: Nietzsche praised him in The Birth of Tragedy (Apollonian/Dionysian) but later attacked him in 'The Case of Wagner' and 'Nietzsche Contra Wagner'
examples: This Schopenhauerian fell out of favor with Nietzsche years after The Birth of Tragedy promoted this thinker's concept of the gesamtkunstwerk.

This man is criticized as 'a symptom of nihilism' in a book subtitled 'A Musician's Problem.' The Case of Wagner was a reversal from Nietzsche's earlier praise of Wagner in a book titled for the 'birth of' tragedy.

clues.clue: Influenced by Schopenhauer; believed opera should be a 'total work of art' in the form of a 'music drama'

works.name: Tristan und Isolde
works.description: Music drama by Wagner, harmonically revolutionary. Famous for the 'Tristan chord' (F, B, D-sharp, G-sharp), which is introduced in the opening bars (cellos: eighth A up to long F, eighth E, long D-sharp). Ends with the soprano Isolde's 'Liebestod' (Mild und leise), in which she asks if she sees how her deceased lover glows. Debussy called it 'decidedly the finest thing I know.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: The 'Tristan chord': the notes F, B, D-sharp, and G-sharp; dissonant and ambiguous; left unresolved until the climactic love-death scene
examples: F, D, G-sharp, and B-sharp comprise a dissonant chord created by this composer that is named for a man whose lover sings the 'Liebestod' aria.

Give this chord named for its role as a Leitmotif for a title character from a harmonically groundbreaking opera. This dissonant, ambiguous chord was daringly left unresolved until the work's climactic 'love-death' scene.

clues.clue: Opening phrase: cellos play an eighth-note A up to a long F, followed by an eighth-note E and a long D-sharp, before a chord of F, B, D-sharp, and G-sharp
examples: An opera by this composer begins with the cellos playing an eighth-note A up to a long F, followed by an eighth-note E and a long D-sharp, before a chord consisting of the notes F, B, D-sharp, and G-sharp.

clues.clue: Ends with Isolde's 'Liebestod' (Mild und leise) — she asks King Marke and Brangäne if they see how her deceased lover glows 'softly and gently'
examples: The soprano lead in that opera asks King Marke and Brangane whether they see how 'softly and gently' her deceased lover glows in her last aria, the Liebestod.

In a recent production, the female title character of an opera by this composer does not die but is instead led away by King Marke after singing 'Mild und leise,' which is traditionally labelled her 'love-death' or Liebestod.

clues.clue: Debussy called it 'decidedly the finest thing I know'; Debussy deployed the Tristan chord humorously in 'Golliwog's Cakewalk' (Children's Corner), instructing 'avec une grande émotion'
examples: Debussy deemed this opera 'decidedly the finest thing I know' after hearing its first act at the 1887 Concerts Lamoureux.
This Impressionist composer humorously deployed Wagner's Tristan chord, instructing to play it 'with great emotion,' in a playful ragtime-inspired piece from Children's Corner.

clues.clue: Brian Magee argues the Tristan chord was an attempt to capture Schopenhauer's ideas about longing and desire
examples: In a monograph about this specific chord, Brian Magee argues that its harmonic concept was an attempt to capture Arthur Schopenhauer's philosophical ideas about longing and desire.

clues.clue: A melody from the Wesendonck Song 'Träume' (Dreams) is referenced in the duet 'O Sink Hernieder'
examples: This composer included 'Traume,' or 'Dreams,' in his Wesendonck Songs, and a melody from that song is referenced in the duet 'O Sink Hernieder' in one of his operas.

works.name: Der Ring des Nibelungen (The Ring Cycle)
works.description: A four-part, approximately 16-hour opera cycle: Das Rheingold, Die Walküre, Siegfried, and Götterdämmerung. Based loosely on the Volsunga saga and Nibelungenlied. The 'Ride of the Valkyries' is its most famous excerpt. Premiered at Bayreuth. Leitmotifs guide plot connections. George Bernard Shaw wrote a Marxist interpretation called 'The Perfect Wagnerite.' Wikipedia

works.name: Bayreuth Festival
works.description: Annual festival in Bavaria dedicated entirely to Wagner's operas, founded by Wagner himself. Premiered Das Rheingold. A 'canon' of 10 works is performed there (excluding Rienzi). After Wagner's death, run by his widow Cosima, then his admirer Winifred, who maintained correspondence with Hitler. Oksana Lyniv became the first woman to take a major conducting role there (2021). Wikipedia

clues.clue: Annual festival in Bavaria dedicated to Wagner's works; the Festspielhaus was built so Wagner could premiere Das Rheingold; leaves out Rienzi
examples: The Festspielhaus was built for this event so its anti-Semitic founder could premiere Das Rheingold.
A 'canon' of 10 works performed at this event leaves out Rienzi.

clues.clue: Winifred Wagner (Wagner family member) maintained correspondence with Hitler and gave the Nazi party control of the festival during WWII
examples: A member of this family named Winifred maintained a lengthy correspondence with Hitler and gave the Nazi party control of her family's opera festival during World War II.
A 2021 Alex Ross book mentions the friendship between the granddaughter of this event's founder, Winifred, and Adolf Hitler; Winifred led this event after the death of its founder's widow Cosima.

works.name: Die Meistersinger von Nürnberg
works.description: Wagner's only mature comedy; over four-and-a-half hours long. Set in Nuremberg. Features the cobbler Hans Sachs, the villain Beckmesser (originally named 'Hanslich' after critic Eduard Hanslick), and a song contest. The C-major overture is densely contrapuntal. A controversial 1956 Bayreuth production by Wieland Wagner featured a giant ball of leaves and flowers. Wikipedia

examples: In this opera, which usually lasts over four-and-a-half hours, the protagonist sings a 'prize song' better than Sixtus Beckmesser does.

clues.clue: Beckmesser's original name was one letter off from critic Eduard Hanslick (originally 'Hanslich')
examples: The original name of this opera's antagonist was one letter off from that of a critic with whom its composer feuded.

clues.clue: Features the aria 'Wahn! Wahn! Überall Wahn!' and a densely contrapuntal C-major prelude combining three themes

examples: A controversial 1956 production of this opera featured a giant ball of leaves and flowers hanging over the characters' heads.

works.name: Tannhäuser
works.description: Opera featuring a song contest at Wartburg between the title character and Wolfram von Eschenbach. Includes a 'Pilgrims' Chorus' (Act III) and an Act II aria about a 'beloved hall' ('Dich teure Halle'). The title character's staff sprouts leaves at the end. Added a bacchanale to Act I for the Paris premiere, which was met with noisemakers and cat-calling. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Song contest at Wartburg between the title character and Wolfram von Eschenbach; includes a 'Pilgrims' Chorus'; ends with the title character's staff sprouting leaves
examples: A song contest at Wartburg is held in this opera between the title character and Wolfram von Eschenbach. This opera includes a 'Pilgrim's Chorus' and ends after the title character's staff sprouts leaves.
Act 3 of [this] opera by this composer includes the title character's hymn to the Evening Star as well as a 'Pilgrims' Chorus.'

examples: Adding a bacchanale to Act I and removing an Act II solo for Walther were two of the amendments that this composer made to an opera for its Paris premiere, which was met with noisemakers and cat-calling from the audience.

clues.clue: Female protagonist (Elisabeth) repeatedly sings 'I greet you!' at the end of an Act II aria about a 'beloved hall'
examples: The female protagonist of that opera by this composer repeatedly sings 'I greet you!' at the end of an Act 2 aria about a 'beloved hall.'

works.name: Lohengrin
works.description: Opera based on the legend of Lohengrin, a knight of the Round Table. Features the 'Bridal Chorus' ('Here Comes the Bride'). Telramund accuses Elsa of Brabant of murder; Elsa dies after asking the title knight his name. Wagner used an onstage ensemble with three piccolos, six horns, a tam-tam, and a wind machine for the Act III storm. Birgit Nilsson made her Bayreuth debut as Elsa. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Telramund accuses Elsa of Brabant of murder; Elsa dies after asking the title knight his name; includes the 'Bridal Chorus'
examples: Telramund accuses the Duchess of Brabant of murder, and Elsa dies after asking the title knight of Arthur his name; this work includes his 'Bridal Chorus.'
Birgit Nilsson performed as Elsa in this composer's Lohengrin in her debut at the Bayreuth Festival.

clues.clue: Chaplin used the orchestral prelude from Lohengrin in The Great Dictator for Hynkel's dance with a balloon globe and the Jewish barber's climactic speech
examples: Chaplin chose the orchestral prelude from this opera to play during both the dictator Hynkel's dance with a balloon globe and the Jewish barber's climactic speech.

works.name: Parsifal
works.description: Wagner's last opera, based on the Arthurian knight Parsifal. Features the villain Klingsor, whom Debussy referenced by calling Wagner 'old Klingsor.' Franz Strauss (father of Richard Strauss) premiered it. Wagner's falling out with Nietzsche was supposedly related to the opera's Christian themes. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Wagner's falling out with Nietzsche may have been related to this opera advancing Christian themes
examples: This philosopher had a falling out with another after discussing his idea for a work titled Parsifal that would advance Christian themes.

clues.clue: Debussy referred to Wagner as 'old Klingsor' after the villain of Parsifal
examples: Debussy referred to this composer of Tristan und Isolde as 'old Klingsor' after the villain of his Parsifal.

works.name: The Flying Dutchman
works.description: Early opera by Wagner. The protagonist Senta ignores the nagging of her nurse Mary and sings 'Traft ihr das Schiff' (her namesake ballad) to a chorus of young girls at their spinning wheels. Uses an onstage ensemble (storm in Act III) including three piccolos, six horns, tam-tam, and wind machine. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Senta ignores nurse Mary and sings 'Traft ihr das Schiff' (Senta's Ballad) to girls at their spinning wheels
examples: In that opera by this composer, the protagonist ignores the nagging of her nurse Mary and sings 'Traft ihr das Schiff,' her namesake ballad, to a chorus of young girls at their spinning wheels.

comprehensive_summary:
Richard Wagner (1813–1883) was a German composer who transformed opera into what he called the Gesamtkunstwerk ('total work of art') — a unified fusion of music, drama, poetry, and visual art. His ideas were developed in the essays 'Art and Revolution,' 'The Artwork of the Future,' and 'Opera and Drama.' He introduced the concept of the leitmotif (recurring musical phrase representing a character or idea), which he advocated for in 'Opera and Drama.' He was deeply influenced by the philosophy of Arthur Schopenhauer. Franz Liszt was his father-in-law (Cosima was Liszt's daughter and Wagner's wife); his son-in-law Houston Stewart Chamberlain wrote The Foundations of the Nineteenth Century, which drew on Wagner's racial theories. Wagner wrote the virulently antisemitic essay 'Jewishness in Music,' attacking Giacomo Meyerbeer and Felix Mendelssohn. He is buried at the estate 'Wahnfried' (meaning 'peace from delusion'), next to his Newfoundland dog Russ; Leonard Bernstein joked that his grave was just large enough to dance upon.

Wagner had a famous relationship with Friedrich Nietzsche, who praised him in The Birth of Tragedy (invoking the Apollonian and Dionysian balance) but later repudiated him in 'The Case of Wagner' and 'Nietzsche Contra Wagner,' calling him a 'symptom of nihilism' and a 'Cagliostro of modernity.' The falling out may have been precipitated by Parsifal's Christian themes. George Bernard Shaw wrote a Marxist interpretation of the Ring Cycle called 'The Perfect Wagnerite,' and Alex Ross wrote the 2020 book Wagnerism: Art and Politics in the Shadow of Music.

Wagner designed the 'Wagner tuba' to fill the acoustic gap between horns and trombones; Bruckner was the first to use it in a symphonic setting (his seventh symphony). Bruckner idolized Wagner, dedicated his Third Symphony to him, quoted Die Walküre and Tristan in it, and represented Wagner's death with a cymbal crash in his seventh symphony.

Tristan und Isolde is his most harmonically revolutionary work, famous for the 'Tristan chord' (F, B, D-sharp, G-sharp), introduced in the opening bars (cellos: pickup A, long F, pickup E, long D-sharp). The chord is dissonant and ambiguous, left unresolved until the climactic 'Liebestod' (Mild und leise), in which Isolde asks King Marke and Brangäne if she sees how her deceased lover glows 'softly and gently.' Debussy called it 'decidedly the finest thing I know' and humorously deployed the chord in 'Golliwog's Cakewalk' (instructed 'avec une grande émotion'). Conducting the opera was the occasion of death for both Joseph Keilberth and Felix Mottl.

The Ring Cycle (Der Ring des Nibelungen) is a four-part, approximately 16-hour cycle (Das Rheingold, Die Walküre, Siegfried, Götterdämmerung), based loosely on the Volsunga saga (omitting the Atli story). Its most famous excerpt, 'The Ride of the Valkyries,' underscores the helicopter attack on a Vietnamese village in Apocalypse Now. Leitmotifs guide plot connections; Hans von Wolzogen coined their common nicknames; the Siegfried leitmotif is a French horn call. Some leitmotifs originated from the Siegfried Idyll, written for Cosima's birthday.

Die Meistersinger von Nürnberg is Wagner's only mature comedy (over four-and-a-half hours), featuring a song contest in which the protagonist (coached by cobbler Hans Sachs) defeats Sixtus Beckmesser (whose original name 'Hanslich' was one letter off from critic Eduard Hanslick). The densely contrapuntal C-major overture combines three themes; the aria 'Wahn! Wahn! Überall Wahn!' laments a riot caused by Beckmesser's interrupted serenade. Tannhäuser features a song contest at Wartburg between the title character and Wolfram von Eschenbach, a 'Pilgrims' Chorus,' and ends with the title character's staff sprouting leaves. Its Paris premiere was disrupted by noisemakers after Wagner added a bacchanale to Act I. Lohengrin features the 'Bridal Chorus' and ends after Elsa of Brabant dies, having asked the title knight his name; Charlie Chaplin used the Lohengrin prelude in The Great Dictator for Hynkel's globe-dance and the Jewish barber's speech. Parsifal's villain Klingsor gave Debussy a name for Wagner.

Wagner founded the Bayreuth Festival in Bavaria, where his operas (except Rienzi) are staged annually. After his death, it was run by his widow Cosima, then Winifred Wagner, who maintained close ties with Hitler and handed the festival to the Nazis during WWII. The 1976 centenary production of the Ring Cycle by Patrice Chéreau was Shaw-inspired socialist realism; Frank Castorf enraged audiences in 2013 by theming the Ring around oil. Oksana Lyniv became the first woman to take a major conducting role there in 2021.

cards.front: Composer: coined the term Gesamtkunstwerk ('total work of art') in essays 'Art and Revolution' and 'The Artwork of the Future'; developed the concept of the leitmotif in 'Opera and Drama'
Composer: wrote the antisemitic essay 'Jewishness in Music,' attacking Giacomo Meyerbeer and Felix Mendelssohn

Composer: Friedrich Nietzsche praised him in The Birth of Tragedy but later attacked him in 'The Case of Wagner' and 'Nietzsche Contra Wagner,' calling him a 'symptom of nihilism' and 'Cagliostro of modernity'
Composer: father-in-law was Franz Liszt (Cosima was Liszt's daughter); son-in-law Houston Stewart Chamberlain wrote The Foundations of the Nineteenth Century
Composer: designed the Wagner tuba to bridge the gap between French horns and trombones; Bruckner first used it symphonically in his seventh symphony
Composer: Bruckner dedicated his Third Symphony to him and included a cymbal crash representing his death in the seventh symphony; Bruckner's Third liberally quotes his operas
Composer: buried at the estate 'Wahnfried' (meaning 'peace from delusion') next to his Newfoundland dog Russ; Leonard Bernstein joked his grave was just large enough to dance upon
Composer: George Bernard Shaw wrote 'The Perfect Wagnerite' (Marxist interpretation of the Ring); Alex Ross wrote Wagnerism: Art and Politics in the Shadow of Music (2020)
Composer: 'Ride of the Valkyries' underscores the helicopter attack in Apocalypse Now; Lohengrin prelude used in The Great Dictator for Hynkel's globe-dance
Opera: features the 'Tristan chord' (F, B, D-sharp, G-sharp) — dissonant and left unresolved until the climactic love-death scene
Opera: opens with cellos playing an eighth A up to a long F, then eighth E, long D-sharp, before the dissonant title chord
Opera: ends with Isolde's 'Liebestod' (Mild und leise) — she asks King Marke and Brangäne if she sees how her deceased lover glows 'softly and gently'
Opera: Debussy called it 'decidedly the finest thing I know'; Debussy humorously quoted its opening chord in 'Golliwog's Cakewalk' (marked 'avec une grande émotion')
Opera: approximately 16-hour, four-part cycle (Das Rheingold, Die Walküre, Siegfried, Götterdämmerung); leitmotifs guide plot connections; Hans von Wolzogen coined their common nicknames
